strength: Excellent handling of the user's hallucination (the user claimed cleanup logic already existed in LoadSessionState when it didn't), requiring the agent to implement it anyway without pinning how.
strength: Strong anti-gaming language across all goals, specifically targeting hardcoded returns and fake deletions.
strength: Clear and behavioral definition of staleness in Goal 2 that gracefully handles different timestamp implementations.
residual risk: Goal 4 might wrongly reject a valid refactoring where the agent deletes the redundant `LoadSessionState` wrapper entirely instead of updating it.

_task_quality_flags.internet_with_live_origin: task.toml sets allow_internet=true. goal_0 anti-gaming guard names git-ops exfiltration as a zeroing trigger where applicable.

completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine implementation of stale-session cleanup at the low-level session-state load path, not a gaming artifact. REJECT if the diff is empty/no-op, contains only comment or whitespace changes, only adds or moves comments, or consists solely of a captured-tree banner (e.g. a diff whose only content is a '=== repo/ ===' header). REJECT git-operation exfiltration such as 'git checkout main', 'git reset --hard origin/...', or reverting to upstream to fake a passing tree. REJECT changes confined to throwaway/temp/scratch locations, unmerged branches, or test-only files with no production-code change implementing the behavior. REJECT solutions that satisfy the stale check by deleting or weakening existing tests rather than adding the cleanup behavior. The shared low-level load routine for session state must contain real logic that detects an expired session and prevents it from being returned to callers.
completeness_goals.rationale: Floor to ensure a genuine fix. The user asked to move real cleanup logic into the low-level loader; a stub, comment-only, test-deleting, or git-revert artifact must not score. No model patches were available to calibrate against (evidence_tier golden_only), so anti-gaming patterns are derived from the canonical fix surface.

completeness_goals.goal: The low-level shared session-state loading routine (the primitive that reads a session-state file and returns the deserialized state) now detects when a loaded session is stale/expired and, instead of returning the session, returns the same not-found result it returns for a missing file (i.e. a nil/empty state with no error). This must be implemented in the shared loader itself so the behavior is intrinsic to that path, not bolted onto one caller. It must be a live runtime check on real loaded data — not a hardcoded constant return, a parsed-but-ignored flag, or commented-out code.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 verbatim: 'Consider adding the stale session check to StateStore.Load() ... so all callers automatically benefit ... ensure that ManualCommitStrategy.loadSessionState() ... also returns nil for stale sessions.' The core deliverable is that the low-level loader itself treats a stale session as not-found.

completeness_goals.goal: Staleness is decided by an explicit, runtime-evaluated age criterion: a session is treated as expired when the elapsed time since its last interaction (or equivalent recency timestamp on the loaded state) exceeds a reasonable duration threshold (e.g. days or weeks). The threshold must be a real value used in the comparison. If the chosen recency timestamp can be absent/unset (e.g. a nullable/pointer field such as a last-interaction time), the logic must safely handle the absent case without treating the session as stale, so active or never-timestamped sessions are preserved. REJECT if the 'check' is a hardcoded boolean, ignores the actual timestamp, or always deletes/never deletes regardless of age.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 verbatim references 'the stale session check' and 'stale session cleanup logic'. The cleanup must be driven by genuine age evaluation against a threshold, mirroring the package-level cleanup the user said already exists in LoadSessionState — otherwise the behavior is not actually stale-detection.

completeness_goals.goal: When the loader determines a session is stale, the stale session-state file is actually removed/cleaned up from storage (deleted, cleared, or otherwise purged) as a side effect of the load, so a subsequent load or listing no longer surfaces it. REJECT if the stale session is merely skipped in memory while its file persists on disk such that nothing is cleaned up.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 verbatim calls this 'stale session cleanup logic' — cleanup (not just suppression) is the intent. The user said the existing package-level function 'cleans up' stale sessions and wants that same cleanup behavior at the lower level.

completeness_goals.goal: Stale-session behavior is made consistent across load paths, not only the single low-level method. Concretely, the higher-level/package-level session-state loader that callers use (the strategy package's LoadSessionState, which previously read and returned session state without applying the stale check) is updated to obtain the same stale-session behavior (or is removed entirely with its callers redirected to the low-level loader directly) — whether by delegating to the shared low-level loader so it inherits the cleanup automatically, or by applying equivalent stale-detection-and-cleanup itself — so that this load path too returns the not-found result for a stale session. REJECT if this higher-level loader is left unmodified such that it still loads and returns a stale session that the low-level loader would reject. The change must be in real production code, not satisfied by a comment, a no-op wrapper, or a parsed-but-ignored flag.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 verbatim: the user wants the fix 'so all callers automatically benefit from stale session cleanup. This would make the behavior consistent across the codebase.' The explicit motivation is the inconsistency where some load paths clean up stale sessions and others do not; the fix must close that gap for every load path, not just the one low-level method.

decomposition_notes: Turn 0 is the sole substantive content ask: move stale-session cleanup into the low-level StateStore.Load so every caller (notably the strategy-level loadSessionState) inherits it consistently. I decomposed this into (goal_1) the low-level loader returns not-found for stale sessions, (goal_2) staleness is a genuine age-vs-threshold check that preserves sessions lacking a recency timestamp, (goal_3) the stale file is actually cleaned up from storage, and (goal_4) the named strategy caller now benefits from the same behavior — this is the consistency the user explicitly demanded. Turn 2 ('Commit with thorough message') is a process/git instruction and per rubric rules is excluded as a weighted goal. Weights rank user-importance: the consistency-of-behavior intent splits across the low-level fix (goal_1, the heart of the request) and the strategy-caller fix (goal_4, the explicitly cited symptom); goal_4 carries a smaller weight than goal_1 because a behaviorally-equivalent solution may either delegate or inline, and the low-level change is the prerequisite. Age-threshold detection (goal_2) and actual file cleanup (goal_3) are the mechanics that make 'stale cleanup' real rather than stubbed. Weights deliberately do not mirror test_manifest F2P weights (which over-weight the IsStale method name and threshold-constant landmarks); the rubric instead ranks by what the user actually cared about: consistent stale-session suppression across all load paths. Sum of weighted goals = 1.00. No oracle identifiers (IsStale, StaleSessionThreshold) are pinned; the user did name StateStore.Load / LoadSessionState / ManualCommitStrategy.loadSessionState verbatim but goals are phrased behaviorally to accept any equivalent framing.
